speaker: interviewer
text: So I know why you picked the name Gardner. But it is also close to Gruenfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you seen the movie,

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh,

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You should see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can rent that movie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Really?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's about a person by the name of Chauncey Gardiner.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's written by Jerzy Kosinski.

speaker: interviewee
text: Really?

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful movie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also the-- go on, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Going back to Gunskirchen for one more moment, another horrible sight that I don't believe I'll live long enough to forget was that one day, the three of us were walking-- saw a corpse where the buttock was cut out. And we saw a guy running with the piece of flesh. Actually, there we saw cannibalism for the first and only time. So it only shows that human beings are not much higher than animals when it comes down to instinct.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Americans now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're in the magazine, eating the food.

speaker: interviewee
text: I grabbed bread, and I was walking out. [LAUGHS] And as soon as I got to the door, somebody grabbed [LAUGHS] the bread out of my hand. So I went back. Going back again was risking your life because the horde was pressing. So I became a little smarter, and broke the bread in half, and put it in the bottom of my pants, and walked out with it. And finally-- oh, and a piece of-- there were blocks of this fat in the blocks, frozen. And [LAUGHS] on my other-- and we had a feast. Of course, the next morning we had a diarrhea that nobody can describe.

words.text: there were blocks of this fat

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your first sight of the Americans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a jeep that I have seen. There were people who saw them earlier, some others. And a very tall guy was yelling, screaming in Hungarian that stay put. Don't move. We're going to bring you food. And nobody paid attention. And they were running like a horde of cattle. He was shooting his pistol in the air. And again, he seemed, to me, 10 feet tall, standing on the jeep. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My uncle that I was together with from the first labor on, he actually-- we couldn't stop his diarrhea. Because the Americans came in without any preparation of-- they did not know how to handle it. What they did, they did bring in food, but also some canned whatever it was, which was not fit for these empty stomachs that were not used to food anymore. And people were dying. 10 times as many as before, from this eating. We were trying to take control, especially this younger uncle of mine who came to last. And he saw the diarrhea, so we had nothing. No medication. He took wood and burned it because he knew, if you burned it crisp, it will sort of stop diarrhea. And we ate that, but for my other uncle, nothing helped. So there was nothing else to do, but called the Americans because he had not much confidence in them. We saw how they handled the sick. Not because they did not try to, but they had no experience whatsoever, and no medication, no facilities. They were putting those who wanted to be evacuated to a hospital on a flat bed truck and carried them away. And we never saw them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we put up my uncle. My other uncle wanted to go with him. They did not let him. And that was the last we saw of him. Which uncle? Ferenc?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ferenc. The one who was with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: The kosher uncle.

speaker: interviewee
text: So again, the irony of he survives and then dies of diarrhea or whatever. After that, finally, a few days later, I got into the typhoid fever. And my uncle, the remaining one, the younger one, this time, he just did not let himself be thrown away-- thrown off from the truck. He came with me and they took me to Horsching. That was the name of it. It was a police school before. And that is what became an infirmary for the survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Austria. This is all in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. Mauthausen on-- everything was in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Gunskirchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gunskirchen in Austria. Near Linz and Wels.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to the hospital now because you had typhoid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was in the hospital. And in the hospital, my uncle was looking after me because the doctors and the nurses were all Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germans. They were under American supervision. They probably tried their best, but still, my uncle did not trust them. He was looking over me like a hawk.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know this uncle's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is [INAUDIBLE] Moshe-- Moshe. Moshe.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's the one that's still alive in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- OK, so you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: In this camp, I remember they put us up on bunk beds, three layers. And after what I had seen-- as my uncle put me up on the top bunk because the lower ones were throwing up, and whatever, and discharge. [LAUGHS] The one under. And for the 24 hours, I slept about 20 hours. And I was, in my dream, always home, being Friday night or, when I was lucky, a Seder night at home. And I was there for five or six weeks. And with my uncle staying with me. He could have gone home, but he didn't. And this is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You recovered your strength?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that picture is after I had arrived in Hungary. It was made in Hungary already. It was probably in mid July or so of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for six weeks, you were in this hospital in Horsching, being cared for by Germans under American supervision.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they came-- the Bricha, the-- the-- this Palestinian group. And they wanted, especially the young ones, to persuade us to go to Palestine. But I had an obsession that I have to go home because my parents are there. [SOBBING] I saw whatever happened, but it could not happen to my parents. They will be waiting for me. And my uncle sort of told me, we should go, too. I wouldn't hear of it. So we wanted to go back to Hungary. They put us on a train, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but I have to go back again for one more moment. On the day of the liberation in Gunskirchen, it was late afternoon. It was still dusk, but you could still see. All of a sudden, we saw those Sonderkommando children because they were still in the striped uniform. They were running into the camp. One had a [NON-ENGLISH], but her leg was still in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: A boot?

speaker: interviewee
text: A [NON-ENGLISH]. A German [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, German boot.

speaker: interviewee
text: But her leg was still in it. Another one with her torso. And that was the sergeant from Mauthausen, so that was the only, only [NON-ENGLISH], the revenge that I have seen that was taken. And also, I should say that the next day, when the Americans came in, they gathered some of the SS-- former SS guards who were hiding in the forests. They caught them, but they were already in civilian suits. But they had the tattoo. You know, the SS had a tattoo on their arms with the death head. And they gathered them, and they were slapping the corpses into the mass graves, like we used to, before. And I saw, with my own eyes, that some of the Americans did the same thing. They kicked them into the grave, like they used to do with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a little-- you know, this liberating army was a different army than the occupying army that later came. They saw, with their own eyes, what was going on. And they had a certain hatred for the Germans. So-- In-- Can I just interrupt real quick? A minute-- The kids-- you called them the Sonder kids, who were doing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sonderkommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the feeling to these-- to these young men? I mean, obviously, they weren't doing it from choice, but did you have an antagonism toward them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, yes. You know, we did not realize then. And again, there was no thinking, and no rationalizing, no logic, no rhyme, reason-- only instinct. We were afraid of them because they were strong. And if we had any food and they saw it, they grabbed it. They took it. But these were people who were dehumanized to the maximum.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war, when they were amongst the other Jews again and you were all liberated, was there-- were they shunned?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Polish. They went back. I had no contact with them. OK. Two episodes. After we declared that we wanted to go back to Hungary, they put us on a train-- the Americans. And we went towards Hungary from Austria. On the way, we started to talk among each other. Who are you? Who are you? Everybody. And one guy says whatever his name was. Joe Klein from Debrecen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another guy goes, Debrecen? Yeah, I know a Joe Klein. Where did you live? So he gives him a street number. He looks at him. He says, you are not that? Who are you? It turned out that he was a Hungarian Gendarme who escaped to the West, picked up one of the dead-- after the liberation, they issued some certificates.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that guy died, and this guy took his certificate, and declared himself to be that guy, and came back to Hungary because he was afraid to go on with his own identity because he was a multiple murderer. So at that point, they grabbed him, took him out of the train, and under the wheels. Finished. This is the extent of what animals we were at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were part of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did have enough strength [LAUGHTER] at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Otherwise, you would have. [INTERPOSING VOICES] You said there were two episodes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other one-- while we were in Horsching, there were next to us, just separated with a chain link fence, another group of people were still in the Hungarian Gendarme uniform. These Hungarian Gen-- the Gendarme was the same in Hungary as the SS in Germany. Next to us, Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in the hospital where, at that point, I was already walking outside, too, and allowed. And there was a gate, also, between us that they could open from their side. And they came in, at will, to our side. And they locked it. And one morning, one of the rabbis-- [SIGHS] because even then, people were dropping like flies, dead-- and the rabbi said, OK, we should make a minyan and say at least a Kaddish out in the open area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they formed a minyan. They davened and said Kaddish. Then comes over a group of these Gendarmes. They said, you, Jews, you still did not learn? You did not have enough? You still continue your religious preaching here? And when that-- that happened, these guys were still weak. They could not do anything on their own, but they went to the American commander of this group. And his name is James Bing. He was a lieutenant. A guy-- strapping, high guy. And we told him the story. He says, OK. He said to this group who came, come with me and point out, to me, who were these guys? And he went over there. And they brought into him half a dozen of these Gendarmes. So he took out his pistol, first of all. His pistol-- with all six of them in front of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he shot in the air. And he said, if any of you ever open your mouth, I will personally kill you. So this was the other episode that I wanted to say, but this, again, was still the liberating army, not the occupying. So when we arrived with this train to [NON-ENGLISH], that was occupied by the Russians. The Americans had to get off, and the Russians came in. But by then, we had little things gathered. The Joint Distribution gave us some clothing. We had some tchotchkes and little things. You know, this and that. And everybody was jealous. Russians came in, and they said, everybody gets off of this train. The engine has to get some water, and then we'll bring it back. So everybody got off. And leave everything there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took [LAUGHS] the train. We never saw [LAUGHS] any of our things. It didn't amount to much, but nevertheless, this was our first introduction to our Russian liberators. [LAUGHTER] Whatever little we had, they took. And then they put us-- until then, we were traveling in a passenger train. [LAUGHS] The Russians put us in a freight train, not like the Germans used to. It had some benches and what not, but-- and they took us back to Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we arrived to Hungary at night-- middle of the night. They told us we cannot leave the train station because there's a curfew. The Russians occupied Budapest. And whoever got out on the street, they stripped them naked. [LAUGHS] The Russians were unbelievable in horde. These were the Mongolians. This was already the occupying army, not the fighting Russians. They [LAUGHS] brought in the Mongolians. And jokes are being told to them, but it is the truth that I was there, personally, when one of them came into a watchmaker, brought in a huge clock, and he said-- they were maniac. They were-- they were-- obsession with the wrist watches for them. He brought in a big clock, and he said, make me six wrist watches [LAUGHTER] from this. Now they're telling it as a joke, but that was a fact. They raped women from 6 to 70. [LAUGHS] So this was our introduction to Hungary. And-- and finally, in the morning, I had an uncle and aunt living in Budapest. And we went to their house, hoping and still waiting for the good news that our parents-- and we did not know then. My aunt had a daughter and a son. The son came back. And after liberation, died in her hands. They say it now that it was meningitis that was rampant in Hungary after the war. And she went berserk, that woman. She actually went crazy. So when we arrived, that was a few days after they buried her son. When we arrived and they saw us, she started to scream, your mother, Margit, is dead. Your father, Simon, is dead. Nobody's alive. My son is dead. Everybody is dead. So I thought I went nuts, too. Crazy. And this was the introduction to the welcome home to Hungary. [SIGHS] And we stayed in Hungary for one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Budapest for six weeks or so. I was still not very strong.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to Gyongyos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later. But while I was in Budapest, that oldest brother of mine who was liberated in Mauthausen, he came home with a heart problem because of the high fever. We learned it later that his valves were gone. And he lived until '86, but, off and on, he was in the hospitals. He was in one hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: He lived till he was 86? Or till 1986?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. In Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this-- Budapest. He never left Hungary after he went back. And after that, I went back to Gyongyos. I went, and to tell you the truth, at that time, I did not have much faith in God. After this aunt episode, then I said, oh, I don't want to have anything to do. Whatever I believed is-- is gone. If there is a God, he's impotent or very cruel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't want anything to do with it. And yet, when I went down to Gyongyos, I said, about me, I do whatever I want, but I have to go to the shul and say a Kaddish for my parents because that, I [? wanted ?] So I wanted to go to the shul. And the orthodox shul in Gyongyos was on a little hill. And there were some 12, 14 steps to go up on the hill. And as I opened the door to the shul, I am faced with the behinds of horses. These Russian son of a guns, they did it on purpose to denigrate any-- but they did that with the churches, too, I have to admit. And you know how uncomfortable to schlep up horses on a hill, on steps? And they did it just to the show how much think-- so I went into that shul. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And where the sacred Torahs used to be kept, there was a huge manure-- bunch of manure. Horses were defecating, urinating where my Rabbi Yungrais used to daven. I had enough of it there, and I said, never ever again I want to believe in God because there isn't any.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet, now you are orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't help it. When the children come, what can you tell them?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you mean between the time that this happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I first came here, one of the reasons that I changed my name was that I wanted to get away from it all. OK. So in Gyongyos, I came back. One of the main purposes was that I wanted to find somebody who can tell me something concrete about my family. Not much after, I found out about my brother. I met that person whom he nurtured back to life and he left him. And I spat him in his face. And I told him what I think of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: When he told you that he left him, was he ashamed?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he did not tell me. Somebody else told me about it. But he did not deny it. He said, I had to come home. I wanted to meet up with my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. He was one of his buddies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and then I was asking and looking for somebody who knew something about my parents. And they told me that my mother was last seen going with a girl named Martha Kahan. And I knew her very well. And she told me that, from the ghetto, her mother was caught in Budapest when the Germans came in and sent right away into a-- she was interned, right then and there. Much earlier than the ghetto was even established in Gyongyos.

words.text: and sent right away into a--

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was left alone with her father. And in the ghetto, after I left, my mother sort of took her under her arm because she was a young girl, alone. And they went in the same wagon and on the train to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Auschwitz, they went in front of Mengele together. And Martha was sent to the right and my mother to the left. So she was an eye witness that my mother died. [CRYING] And I married that girl. She was my first wife. In 1946, October, we left Hungary because we did not feel that we could live there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any trouble leaving Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we escaped on a Russian truck. We bribed the driver, who had to go anyhow to Austria. And he took us over to the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were married already?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Her brother, myself, and a bunch of other people went over to Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is now 1940--

speaker: interviewee
text: '46 October.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were 21 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same age. Same age. We were born in the same year.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were taken to-- you went you-- you escaped to Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Vienna for awhile. We were in the Rothschild Hospital for a week. And then we went to Salzburg and then to Stuttgart, near Stuttgart, in Schwabisch Hall. And we were there and in Ulm for three years. And exactly three years later, in October 1949, we arrived to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in these places in Austria? Were you in displaced persons camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, in Austria, in Salzburg. And then in Germany, in Schwabisch Hall. And in Germany, in Ulm.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were all DP camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: All DP camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and did you-- did you do any work there or did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the DP camp, first, we went to study radio technique, radio installation, and whatnot. And my late wife, she's-- she went to-- these are all true [? art ?] schools. She did shirt making. And then we all thought that within six months, we would all be in America. We had affidavits. What more [LAUGHS] can you ask for? And it dragged out for three years. And then we both worked in the UNRRA first.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: UNRRA. It was administrating the DP camps. And in 1948, I believe, the IRO took over from the UNRRA.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the International Relief--

speaker: interviewee
text: International Refugee Organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the meantime, of course, while we were in Germany, there was the establishment of the state of Israel. And at this point, our whole life, after the war, was aimless, useless. We felt that this-- how can you live with these memories? How can you live a normal life being alone? Not to have a parent, brothers-- once we counted, we lost just grandparents, uncles, aunts, cousins. Some 87 of them. So-- [SIGHS] And this went on until 1948. When the state of Israel was established, it gave us a new life. We felt, even though the Arabs [LAUGHS] or the Israeli Jews don't like to hear it, from the ashes of the 6 million, Israel was established. Then, somehow, we accepted the fact that there is some justice in this world. That something came out from this horrible tragedy of the humankind. After 2,000 years, we have a land, a country. And had there been one in the '30s, in the early '40s, who knows how many more Jews would be alive today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you consider going to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, we did. We sent our belongings to Israel, bec-- frankly, not because we wanted so much to go, but because it was so hopeless to wait for America, it seemed, at one time. But after, again, because we became complacent, that, oh, we suffered enough, we want to live [LAUGHS] a little-- I should say that one of my brother in-laws went to Palestine in '46, '47. And he kept on writing us to the camp-- don't come here. Life is very, very difficult. And so on and so on. But when it seemed, after four years, that we can't make it, we sent our belongings. [LAUGHS] And yet, we stayed behind. So-- but after the establishment of the state of Israel, even though we did not go to Israel we felt that something is worthwhile to continue to live. If, for nothing else, to help Israel. And again--
